13: hazeh haveit bechezwe leylaya vaara im-anane shemayya kebar enash ateh vead attiq yomaya matah uqdamoy haytivuhi (Aramaic): 'I saw in the night visions, and behold, with the clouds of heaven there came one like a son of man, and he came to the Ancient of Days and was presented before him.' The 'one like a son of man' (kebar enash, Aramaic for 'like a human being') in Daniel 7 contrasts with the four beasts (lions, bears, leopards, a terrible beast) that rise from the sea — representing successive human empires. The human figure comes from heaven, not the sea, and receives the dominion the beasts claimed. The NT application (Jesus's self-designation as 'Son of Man' in all four Gospels) is the consistent claim that Jesus is this figure who receives eternal dominion from the Ancient of Days — a claim recognized as divine by the Sanhedrin (Mark 14:62-64).
1: ḥĕlem ḥĕzî rēʾšēh (חֵלֶם חֶזְוֵי רֵאשֵׁהּ): 'a dream and visions of his head.' Ḥĕlem (dream, Aramaic) parallels Hebrew חֲלוֹם. Ḥĕzwāʾ (Aramaic, plural: visions) is from the root ḥzh = to see, the standard Aramaic visionary vocabulary. Daniel writes the dream (kĕtab ḥĕlmāʾ) — the narrative self-archiving of the vision, an act of prophetic accountability. The mention of 'chief of the matters' (rêš millîn) signals that Daniel summarizes the dream's essence.
2: ʾarbaʿ rûḥîn dî-šĕmayyāʾ mĕgîḥān (אַרְבַּע רוּחִין דִּי-שְׁמַיָּא מְגִיחָן): 'the four winds of heaven stirring.' Rûḥîn = winds/spirits (Aramaic plural of rûaḥ). Mĕgîḥān from gîḥ = to rush out, burst forth. The 'great sea' (yammāʾ rabbāʾ) is the Mediterranean in the literal sense, but functions symbolically as chaotic primordial waters (Gen 1:2; Isa 27:1). Revelation 7:1 picks up the four winds held back by angels as the same cosmic force that precedes the sealing of God's servants.
3: ʾarbaʿ ḥêwān rabbĕwān sāleqan min-yammāʾ: 'four great beasts came up from the sea.' Ḥêwāh (Aramaic) = beast, living creature — cognate with Hebrew חַיָּה. Beasts rising from the sea echoes the Canaanite chaos-myth (Yam, the sea-dragon) and Isa 27:1 (Leviathan). The four beasts represent four world-empires, confirmed in 7:17: 'These four great beasts are four kings who shall arise out of the earth.'
4: ʾaryēh wĕgappîn dî-nĕšar lēh (אַרְיֵה וְגַפִּין דִּי-נְשַׁר לֵהּ): 'a lion with eagle's wings.' ʾAryēh (lion) + nĕšar (eagle) — Babylon's two royal symbols in both Neo-Babylonian iconography and the OT (Jer 4:13; Hab 1:8). The plucking of wings (mĕrîṭ) and being made to stand like a man (kĕʾĕnāš qāmat) and receiving a human heart (lĕbab ʾĕnāš yĕhîb lēh) tracks with Nebuchadnezzar's humiliation and restoration in chs. 3-4.
5: dāmyāh lĕdubb (דָּמְיָה לְדֹב): 'resembling a bear.' The comparative particle + animal comparison is the standard beast-vision idiom. The bear raised on one side (lĕsitar-ḥad hûqĕmat) is commonly interpreted as Medo-Persia, with one side elevated because Persia dominated Media. The three ribs (tĕlātāh ʿillʿîn) in its mouth are typically identified with three conquered kingdoms; the command 'arise, devour much flesh' captures Persia's vast territorial expansion.
6: gappîn dî-ʿôp ʿal gabbēh (גַּפִּין דִּי-עוֹף עַל-גַּבֵּהּ): 'wings of a bird on its back.' The leopard (nĕmar) with four wings and four heads represents Greece under Alexander and his four successor-generals (the Diadochi) — confirmed by 8:8, 22. The swiftness of Alexander's conquest is captured in the image of a leopard already fast, with four wings added. Šoltān (dominion) was given to it — the same word used for the Son of Man's dominion in 7:14, but here provisional.
7: dĕḥîlāh wĕʾêmtānāh wĕtaqqîpāh yattîrāʾ: 'dreadful, terrible, and exceedingly strong.' Three Aramaic adjectives pile up: dĕḥîl (feared), ʾêmtan (terrifying), taqqîp yattîr (exceedingly mighty). The fourth beast is unnamed — unlike the lion, bear, and leopard, no animal analogy suffices for Rome's universal and unprecedented dominion. It has iron teeth (šinnayyin dî-parzel) and ten horns, and tramples everything.
8: qeren ʾoḥŏrî zĕʿêrāh (קֶרֶן אָחֳרִי זְעֵירָה): 'another horn, a small one.' Qeren (horn, Aramaic and Hebrew) is the standard symbol of power/ruler throughout the OT. The 'little horn' starts small (zĕʿêr) but speaks grandiose things. Eyes like human eyes (ʿênîn kĕʿênê ʾĕnāšāʾ) suggest intelligence; a mouth speaking great things (pum mĕmalil rabrĕbān) is its primary characteristic. This figure forms the background for the NT's 'man of lawlessness' and 'antichrist' traditions.
9: ʿattîq yômayāʾ yĕtib (עַתִּיק יוֹמַיָּא יְתִב): 'the Ancient of Days took his seat.' ʿAttîq (Aramaic) = ancient, advanced in days — a title used only three times in Daniel (7:9, 13, 22) and nowhere else in Scripture. The description that follows — garment white as snow (lĕbûšēh kĕtlag ḥiwr), hair like pure wool (śar rēʾšēh kĕʿamar nĕqēʾ), throne of fiery flames (kursyēh šĕbîbîn dî-nûr), wheels of burning fire — is the direct source for Revelation 1:13-15's description of Christ.
10: nĕhar dî-nûr nāgēd wĕnāpēq min-qŏdāmôhî: 'a stream of fire issued and came out from before him.' The fiery stream (nĕhar dî-nûr) is the river of divine judgment from the throne. Revelation 22:1 transforms this into the river of life flowing from the throne — the same stream carries either judgment or life depending on one's relationship to the enthroned one. The 'ten thousand times ten thousand' standing before him (ribbô ribbĕwān) becomes the number of the heavenly host in Rev 5:11.
11: bĕqōl millayāʾ rabbĕrĕbātāʾ: 'because of the sound of the great words.' The fourth beast's destruction is specifically caused by its speech: the great-sounding mouth is the proximate cause of its slaying. Qĕtal (slain) + yĕqad bĕnûrāʾ (burnt in fire) matches 2 Thess 2:8: the lawless one 'the Lord will consume with the breath of his mouth and bring to nothing by the appearance of his coming.'
12: ʿiddānĕhôn ûzimānĕhôn yĕhalûn: 'their seasons and times were prolonged.' The three defeated beasts have their dominion taken away but their lives extended for 'a season and a time.' ʿIddan (Aramaic) = time, season — the same word used in 7:25 for 'time, times, and half a time.' The contrast between the fourth beast's total destruction and the others' sustained existence (minus dominion) suggests a graduated pattern of divine judgment.
14: šāltān wîqār ûmalkû (שָׁלְטָן וִיקָר וּמַלְכוּ): 'dominion and glory and a kingdom.' Three Aramaic throne-terms given to the Son of Man: šāltān (sovereignty/dominion), yĕqār (honor/glory), malkû (kingdom). The triad reappears in Revelation 5:12 as the acclamation of the Lamb. The universality clause — kol-ʿammayyāʾ ʾummayāʾ wĕlišānayāʾ — is the same tripartite peoples-nations-languages formula from throughout Daniel, now applied to the Son of Man's eternal dominion.
15: ʾitkaryat rûḥî ʾĕnāh dānîyēʾl bĕgaw nidnāh: 'my spirit was anxious within me in its sheath.' Kĕrāh (to be anxious, contracted) describes Daniel's interior state. The word nidnāh (sheath/covering) is debated: most translate 'in its sheath' — the spirit within its bodily covering. The image of the spirit as something held within the body-sheath reflects Aramaic anthropology where rûaḥ is the animating interior person.
16: yaddaʿ ʾôdaʿinnî: 'he made me know definitively.' The doubled causative form of ydʿ (know, Aramaic Aphel) means the angel causes Daniel to know certainly. The pattern — visionary overwhelmed, angelic interpretation follows — is the standard apocalyptic schema developed from Ezekiel 1-2 and formalized here in Daniel 7, influencing Revelation's entire interpretive structure.
17: ʾinnûn ʾarbaʿāh malkîn dî-min-ʾarʿāʾ yĕqûmûn: 'these four great beasts are four kings who shall arise from the earth.' The interpretive key: beasts = kings/kingdoms arising from ʾarʿāʾ (earth). The source is earth (ʾarʿāʾ), in contrast to the Son of Man who comes from heaven's clouds. Earth-origin vs. heaven-descent is the structural contrast of the whole vision.
18: qaddîšê ʿelyônîn (קַדִּישֵׁי עֶלְיוֹנִין): 'the saints of the Most High.' Qaddîš (Aramaic) = holy one, saint — the same word used for angelic 'watchers' (qaddîšîn, 4:13, 17). ʿElyônîn = the Most High (plural of majesty for God in Aramaic). The promise — 'shall receive the kingdom and possess the kingdom forever' — uses the same malkû (kingdom) given to the Son of Man (v. 14). The saints share in what the Son of Man receives; their possession flows from his.
19: dî hăwāt šĕniyyāh min-kol-lĕhon: 'which was different from all the others.' Šĕnāh (Aramaic, passive: to be different/changed) — the fourth beast is qualitatively different, not just another variant. Iron teeth and bronze claws are added to the earlier description; the trampling is emphasized twice. This beast's un-nameable character signals that it exceeds the existing taxonomy of power.
20: qarnāʾ dî-ʿênayyin hăwāt bāh wĕpum mĕmalil rabrĕbān: 'the horn that had eyes and a mouth speaking great things.' The little horn's distinguishing features — eyes (ʿênayyin = intelligence) and mouth (pum mĕmalil) — are cognitive and rhetorical. It is a horn that thinks and speaks rather than merely exercises power. Revelation 13:5-6 describes the beast from the sea with 'a mouth uttering haughty and blasphemous words' — drawing directly from this passage.
21: qarnāʾ dikkēn ʿābĕdāh qĕrāb ʿim-qaddîšîn wĕyāklāh lĕhôn: 'the same horn made war with the saints and prevailed over them.' ʿAbĕdāh qĕrāb (made war) — the warfare vocabulary of Daniel echoes Revelation 13:7: 'It was allowed to make war on the saints and to conquer them.' The prevailing of the little horn over the saints is temporary — the Ancient of Days' arrival (v. 22) ends it — but the interim domination is real and its duration is measured.
22: ʿad dî-ʾătāh ʿattîq yômayāʾ wĕdînāʾ yĕhib lĕqaddîšê ʿelyônîn: 'until the Ancient of Days came and judgment was given in favor of the saints of the Most High.' The Aramaic dînāʾ yĕhib lĕ- means 'judgment was rendered in favor of' — the same phrase in legal contexts means 'a verdict for' the plaintiff. The arrival of the Ancient of Days turns the court verdict: from prevailing horn to holy-ones-vindicated.
23: malkûtāʾ rĕbîʿāytāʾ (מַלְכוּתָא רְבִיעָיְתָא): 'a fourth kingdom on earth.' The angel confirms beast = kingdom. The fourth kingdom will tēʾkul kol-ʾarʿāʾ (devour the whole earth) and tĕdûšinnāh wĕtaddiqinnāh (trample and crush it) — three verbs of total dominance. The scale of the fourth kingdom's destructive power accounts for the unprecedented terror of its symbolic form.
24: wĕʾoḥŏran lēh yĕqûm: 'and another shall arise after them.' The little horn arises ʾoḥĕran (after, different) from the ten, humbles three kings, and is therefore both sequential and qualitatively other. The 'humbling of three kings' (wĕtĕlātāh malkîn yĕšappil) has generated various historical identifications but functions literarily as a marker of unprecedented political disruption within the final empire.
25: ʿiddān wĕʿiddānîn ûpĕlag ʿiddān (עִדָּן וְעִדָּנִין וּפְלַג עִדָּן): 'a time, and times, and half a time.' The most contested chronological formula in Daniel. ʿIddān = time/year (Aramaic); ʿiddānîn = times; pĕlag ʿiddān = half a time. The formula = 1 + 2 + ½ = 3½ units. This corresponds to the 42 months / 1,260 days of Revelation 12:14; 11:2-3; 13:5 — the period of the great tribulation in apocalyptic reckoning. The little horn speaks great words against the Most High and 'wears out the saints' (yĕballē qaddîšîn) during this period.
26: wĕdînāʾ yitib wĕšāltānēh yĕhaddĕmûn: 'but the court shall sit in judgment and his dominion shall be taken away.' The heavenly court that earlier gave judgment in favor of the saints (v. 22) now acts to annihilate the little horn's dominion. Šāltān taken away, consumed, and finally destroyed — three stages: removal of authority, ongoing destruction (yahăbĕdûn), and terminal annihilation (ʿad-sôpāʾ).
27: malkûtāʾ wĕšāltānāʾ wĕrabbûtāʾ: 'the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under the whole heaven.' The third and final occurrence of the throne-vocabulary triad (malkût + šāltān + now rabbût, greatness). 'Under the whole heaven' — the domain previously claimed by the four beasts is now given to the saints. The eternal kingdom (malkûtēh malkût ʿālam) serves the Son of Man; the saints' kingdom is the visible administration of what the Son of Man receives in vv. 13-14.
28: sôpāʾ pĕtgāmāʾ (סוֹפָא פִּתְגָמָא): 'here is the end of the matter.' Sôpāʾ (Aramaic) = end, conclusion. Daniel 7:28 ends the Aramaic block that began at 2:4b — the final verse of the Aramaic section is marked by personal distress (rĕʿyônay yĕballĕhunnannî, 'my thoughts alarmed me') and preserved silence (millĕtāʾ bĕlibbî nĕṭarēt, 'I kept the matter in my heart'). The changed countenance (zîwî šĕnaw ʿălî) is a standard theophanic response.
